Q1
(a) (i)Accounting for pension scheme
A defined benefit scheme fixes the amount of benefit to be paid
to pensioners;usually,as a percentage of employees' final salaries. This means that
the contributions throughout employees' working lives are variable and therefore, the
accounting is not straightforward.
• The entity’s statement of financial position will include a net defined benefit deficit
(or unusually a surplus). This is the difference between the fair value of the assets
in the pension plan and the present value of the obligation to pay future pensions.
This is advised by an actuary and changes from year to year; the change is
recognised in profit or loss and other comprehensive income(OCI).
• The net interest (on the opening deficit or surplus) is recognised in profit or loss.
This represents the return on investments and the unwinding of the discount on the
obligation.
• Current service cost is also recognised in profit or loss. This is the increase to the
obligation resulting from the provision of an additional year's service by employees.
• Remeasurements, which are recognised in OCI,include:
- any actuarial gain or loss for the period resulting from changes in actuarial
assumptions when determining the value of the obligation;and
- the difference between the actual return on assets held and the return that has
been included in profit or loss.
Kostas is therefore incorrect to recognise the cost of remeasurements ($14
million) as an administrative expense in profit or loss.
Kostas is considering reducing the number of employees participating in the defined
benefit scheme. This would be a curtailment and any compensation paid would
be a settlement:
• As a result of the curtailment,the present value of the pension obligation will
reduce and give rise to a positive past service cost, which must be recognised in
profit or loss in the period of the curtailment.
• If the compensation paid (the settlement) is not the same amount as the reduction
in the pension obligation, a gain or loss will arise. This is also recognised in
profit or loss.
The remaining net defined benefit surplus or deficit continues to be accounted for in
line with IAS 19.
The remeasurement of the obligation at the time of the curtailment must be made
using updated assumptions, and these assumptions are also used after the
curtailment to measure the interest cost and current service cost forthe remainder of
the accounting period.
(ii) Adjustment to equity
Kostasheld a 70% controlling interest in Easton prior to the transaction; the sale of
10% of this holding did not constitute a loss of control and therefore Easton continues
to be a subsidiary after the transaction.
As a result, a gain or loss on disposal is not recognised (since no net assets are
disposed of). Instead, the sale of shares is considered to be a transaction between
shareholders; Easton continues to be consolidated on a line-by-line basis, but the NCI
isnow 40% rather than 30%.
The transaction is therefore reflected in equity by increasing the NCI in net assets at
the transaction date and recognising any difference between proceeds and the change
to the NCI in group equity.
The increase to the NCI is calculated as the relevant percentage (here 10%) of the
total carrying amount of the subsidiary at the transaction date. Since the NCI was
measured at fair value at acquisition, this includes both net assets and goodwill.
Goodwill at acquisition is calculated as the difference between consideration paid plus
the NCI at fair value and the fair value of identifiable net assets acquired. As the
carrying amount of net assets was less than their fair valueat acquisition, afair value
adjustment of $100,000 was requiredat. This relates to plantthat is still owned by
Easton at 30 April 20X8. Therefore the net assets at disposal are adjusted for the
depreciated fair value adjustment of $6 million (10m x 3/5 years)
(iii) Financial asset investment
Classification
The bond has been classified as a financial asset at amortised cost. As the bond carries
contractual interest payments on specified dates, this classification is correct assuming
that Kostasintends to hold the asset to collect contractual cash flows (i.e.to hold it to
maturity in April 20Y0).
The bond can only be reclassified to the FVTOCI category if Kostaschanges the
business model within which the asset is held such that it is held for the purpose of
collecting contractual cash flows and selling. Otherwise it cannot be reclassified.
In any case, the financial controller is incorrect to assume that the expected credit loss
(ECL) approach to impairment in IFRS 9 does not apply to a financial asset at
FVTOCI. It applies in the same way as to assets classified as amortised cost, except
that the ECLs are recognised as a balance in OCI and financial assets continue to be
presented gross in the statement of financial position. Financial assets at amortised
cost are presented net of the loss allowance.
Impairment
The interest due on the bond at the end of the reporting period was not received and
this fact was identified during the preparation of the financial statements. Interest due
was paid late in the previous six-month period and it therefore seems indicative of a
financial problem of the issuer. Therefore, this is an adjusting event after the end of
the reporting period and Kostas’s financial statements for the year ended 30 April
20X8 should recognise the impairment of the financial asset.
At initial recognition of the bond investment, Kostas should have recognised
12-month ECLs in line with IFRS 9. When contractual payments are more than 30
days past due there is a rebuttable presumption that the credit risk attached to the asset
has significantly increased.
Therefore, Kostas should recognise a loss allowance equal to lifetime ECLs. This is
the ECLs that would arise if default occurred at any time in the remaining term of the
bond. ECLs are calculated as cash shortfalls (i.e. the difference between amounts
contractually due and what Kostas expects to receive). These are probability weighted
to determine the loss allowance.
Kostas should recognise a loss allowance in profit or loss and present the bond
investment net of this amount. As there was no objective evidence that impairment
existed at the reporting date (30 April 20X8), interest continues to be calculated on the
gross carrying amount of the financial asset.
(b)Business combination in stages
The initial acquisition of a 25% equity stake in Weston Cois likely to lead to
Kostashaving significant influence over it. IAS 28 Investments in Associates
and Joint Ventures states that significant influence exists if an entity
has between 20% and 50% of an equity stake, unless it can be
demonstrated otherwise.
IAS 27 Separate Financial Statements deals with accounting for the equity investment
in Kostas’s separate financial statements, stating that the investment can either be
accounted for:
• At cost;
• In accordance with IFRS 9, which means the investment will be accounted for at fair
value with any changes being taken either to profit or loss (or OCI if the relevant
election is made at initial recognition); or
• By using the equity method prescribed by IAS28.
In the consolidated financial statements, assuming significant influence exists, Kostas
will account for the 25% equity investment using the equity method. In the statement
of financial position the investment will be measured at cost plus a share of any post
acquisition profits less any impairment losses and distributions received. The
statement of profit or loss and OCI will include a share of the associate’s profit for the
year and a share of its OCI.
If Kostas decides to acquire an additional 50%equity stake then from that date Weston
Co will be a subsidiary of Kostas as Kostas will have the power to control the
activities of the subsidiary.
The original 25% holding in Weston Co will be remeasured tofair value at the date the
additional shares are acquired and any gain or loss on remeasurement will be included
in profit or loss.
The assets, liabilities, income and expenses of the entity will be consolidated from the
date on which control is achieved and goodwill will be calculated at this date. The
remaining 25% not held by Kostas will be recognised in the equity section of the
consolidated statement of financial position as an NCI. Subsequently it increases by
the NCI's share of the subsidiary's total comprehensive income

stem_zh:
Q2
(a) Accounting implications
(i) Investment property
The accounting for the investment property will not affect the reported profits of
Heathcliff as a separate entity because it is owned by Linton, but it will affect the
consolidated financial statements. In the separate financial statements of Linton, the
subsidiary, it may be correct to classify the property as investment property and
recognise any gains in fair value in profit or loss; any fall in fair value must also be
recognised in profit or loss. However, when it comes to the consolidated statements,
the property cannot be classified as investment property as it is owner-occupied by
the Heathcliff group. Instead it will be classified as property, plant and equipment
(IAS 16Property, Plant and Equipment).
The property can be revalued if all other assets in the class are revalued, but any
increases in value are presented as other comprehensive income and therefore not
included in profit or loss. If there is a decrease in the value of the asset then that
decrease will be included in profit or loss unless the asset had previously been
revalued upwards. The asset must also be depreciated, even if it has been revalued,
with the expense charged to profit or loss. The suggestion by the accountant is
therefore incorrect.
(ii)Useful life of assets
The determination of the useful life of non-current assets is required in order to
calculate depreciation expense. Depreciation expense meets the IAS 8 definition of
an accounting estimate, being a monetary amount in financial statements subject to
measurement uncertainty. IAS8 Accounting Policies, Changes in Accounting Estimate
and Errors permits changes in accounting estimates as a result of new information,
new developments or more experience.
A change in an input to an accounting estimate, such as useful life, results in a change
in an accounting estimate and should be applied prospectively. Therefore,if the useful
life of Heathcliff’s asset sis judged to have increased, subsequent depreciation will be
recognised over the revised remaining useful life resulting in a lower annual
depreciation charge and higher annual profits. However, useful life should not be
increased without justification, such as technological advances that have increased the
life of the asset.
The trainee’s assertion that no one is bothered about the depreciation charge just
because the asset has been paid for is arguable. Depreciation expense does affect
reported profits; stakeholders rely on reported profits when making decisions relating
to an entity. However, it is true that many analysts will add back depreciation expense
to reported profits when comparing results of entities over time and against other
entities. There may therefore be some justification in the initiative, but it should not
be done just to increase profits.
(iii) Inventory write-down
One of the principles of IAS 2 Inventories is that inventory is measured at the lower
of cost and net realisable value (NRV). If NRV has fallen below cost,inventory must
be written down and any impairment charged to profit or loss in the period of the
write-down. The issue here is the timing of the impairment.
If the decrease in inventory value relates to the previous period and an error was made
in not writing down the inventory in that period, the accountant is correct and the
error should be treated as a prior period error. If the error arose in the year ended 31
December 20X8, the results of that year should be restated when presented as
comparatives in the financial statements for the year ended 31 December 20X9. As a
result, the reported profit for the previous year will be reduced and therefore the
opening retained earnings of the current year will also be reduced. Profit or loss for
the current year will, however, be unaffected.
If the impairment occurred in the current period (e.g. due tofire damage), the
write-down must be made in the current period resulting in a reduction of current
year profit. The statement by the trainee accountant may be correct, but the head
accountant should investigate the timing of the impairment. It cannot be treated as
prior period just to please the chairman and increasehis bonus.
(iv) Loan notes
The significant financial difficulty of the supplier is evidence that the investment in
loan notes is credit impaired on purchase (i.e. events that will have a detrimental
effect on cash flows associated with the financial asset have already occurred).
IFRS 9 Financial Instruments applies and requires that the investment is measured
at fair value at acquisition;however,no loss allowance is initially recognised. This it
the financial asset is credit adjusted (i.e. it takes into account the initial lifetime
expected credit losses in the estimated cash flows). The trainee is therefore incorrect
to suggest that the asset is recognised at nominal value with the discount recognised
as income in profit or loss.
(b)Ethical issues
Ms Smeatonisa member of ACCA, a professional body, and is therefore bound by its
Code of Ethics and Conduct. As a professional accountant, Ms Smeatonis expected
to meet standards of professional behaviour and carry out her duties with due care and
for the benefit of the company as a whole and not individuals within the company.
As Heathcliff prepares accounts in accordance with IFRS Standards, those standards
must be followed. Accounting treatments cannot be selected based on their impact on
profitability. Although IFRS Standards do allow judgement to be applied, that
judgement is not driven by the desires of the chairman of the company. Some
judgement may well be required in the situations given:
•When assessing the useful life of an asset;and
•To identify the actual point in time when the inventories were impaired.
If Ms Smeaton feels pressured into taking actions of an unethical
nature because of the relationship between the trainee and Heathcliff’s
chairman, she should seek assistance initially from her direct superior (e.g.
finance director) and if that does not solve the problem she should turn to a committee
of the board of directors or the board itself.
As the email has now circulated within the company, Ms Smeaton must take a step
back and distance her self from the proposals because the trainee has a clear conflict
of interest. The head accountant should seek to ensure that the correct accounting
treatment is applied in accordance with IFRS Standards , rather than the wishes of the
trainee or the chairman.
Ms Smeaton should consider whether the accounting judgements and decisions are for
the benefit of the company as a whole and not one or two individuals within the
company. Most of the initiatives may well generate greater profits for the company,
but both IFRStandardand the Code oEthics and Conduct must be adhered to. The
trainee accountant may risk losing his professional qualification if it can be
proven that he has acted in an unethical manner. The chairman is also
in breach of his fiduciary duties (e.g. failing to exercise independent
judgement)and having compromised the accountant’s position may be
regarded as an abuse of power.
source_doc: ACCA:sbr:essay:SBR-2022-Pre June-A.pdf#2

stem_zh:
Q3
(a) Borrowing costs
The cost of an item of property, plant and equipment may include borrowing costs
incurred for the purpose of acquiring or constructing it. IAS 23 Borrowing Costs
requires such borrowing costs to be capitalised if the asset takes a substantial period
of time to be prepared for its intended use or sale.
The definition of borrowing costs includes interest expense calculated by the effective
interest method, finance charges on lease borrowings and exchange differences arising
from foreign currency borrowings relating to interest costs.
Borrowing costs should be capitalised during construction and include the costs of
funds borrowed for the purpose of financing the construction of the asset, and general
borrowings which would have been avoided if the expenditure on the asset had
occurred. The general borrowing costs are determined by applying a capitalisation
rate to the expenditure on that asset. The capitalisation rate will be the weighted
average of the borrowing costs applicable to the general pool.
The weighted-average carrying amount of the stadium during the period is $95
million(¼ × $(20 + 70 + 120 + 170)).
The capitalisation rate of the borrowings of Rochester during the period of
construction is 9% per annum, therefore the total amount of borrowing costs to be
capitalised is the weighted-average carrying amount of the stadium multiplied by the
capitalisation rate.That is $2.85 million ($95 *9%*4/12).
(b) Players’ registrations
An intangible asset should be recognised when future economicbenefits are expected
(i.e. probable) and the cost of the asset can be measured reliably(IAS 38 Intangible
Assets). Therefore, the costs associated with the acquisition of players’ registrations
should be capitalised at the fair value of the consideration payable. Capitalised costs
include transfer fees, league levy fees, agents’ fees incurred by the club and other
directly attributable costs. Costs also include the fair value of any contingent
consideration, which is primarily payable to the player’s former club with associated
league levy fees, once payment becomes probable.
Subsequent reassessments of the amount of contingent consideration payable should
be also included in the cost of the player’s registration. The estimate of the fair value
of the contingent consideration payable requires management to assess the likelihood
of specific performance conditions being met, which would trigger the payment of the
contingent consideration.
This assessment should be carried out on an individual player basis. The additional
amount of contingent consideration potentially payable, in excess of the amounts
included in the cost of players’ registrations, should be disclosed. Capitalised costs
should be fully amortised over the period covered by the player’s contract.
Where a playing contract is extended, any costs associated with securing the
extension are added to the unamortised balance at the date of the extension and the
revised carrying amount is amortised over the remaining revised contract life. Player
registrations should be classified as assets held for sale under IFRS 5 Non-current
Assets Held for Sale and Discontinued Operations when their carrying amount is
expected to be recovered principally through a sale transaction and a sale is
considered to be highly probable. Additionally, the registrations should be actively
marketed by Rochester, which it appears that they are. It would appear that in these
circumstances that management is committed to a plan to sell the registration, that the
asset is available for immediate sale, that an active programme to locate a buyer is
initiated by circulating clubs. In order to fulfil the last criteria of IFRS 5, it may be
prudent to only class these registrations as available for sale where unconditional
offers have been received prior to a period end. IFRS 5 requires that it is unlikely
that the plan to sell the registrations will be significantly changed or withdrawn.
However, because of the subjectivity involved, in the case of player registrations these
assets should be stated at the lower of the carrying amount and fair value less costs to
sell, as the carrying amount will already be stated in accordance with IFRS
Standards.Gains and losses on disposal of players’ registrations should be determined
by comparing the fair value of the consideration receivable, net of any transaction
costs, with the carrying amount and should be recognised in profit or loss on the
disposal of players’ registrations. Where a part of the consideration receivable is
contingent on specified performance conditions, this amount is recognised in profit or
loss when the conditions are met.
IAS 36 Impairment of Assets states that entities should annually test their assets for
impairment. An asset is impaired if its carrying amount exceeds its recoverable
amount which is the higher of the asset’s fair value less costs of disposal and its value
in use. It is difficult to determine the value in use of an individual player in isolation
as that player (unless via a sale or insurance recovery) cannot generate cash flows on
his own. Although any individual player cannot really be separated from the single
cash generating unit (CGU)that is the basketball or football team, there may be certain
circumstances in which a player is taken out of the CGU when it becomes clear that
they will not play for the club again(e.g. due to a career threatening injury or
permanent removal from the playing squad for another reason).
If such circumstances arise, the carrying amount of the player should be assessed
against the best estimate of the player’s fair valueless any costs to sell and an
impairment charge made in profit or loss, which reflects any loss arising. The
playing registrations disposed of, subsequent to the year end, for $25 million, with an
associated carrying amount of $7 million, would be disclosed as events after the
reporting date.
(c) Sustainability reporting
Having made the decision to introduce sustainability initiatives into its operations, it
would be beneficial to Rochester to begin report on sustainability in a structured and
effective way.
Investors are increasingly aware of and concerned with sustainability issues and
corporate performance. Many investors, particularly funds, specifically choose to
invest ethically, in companies that reflect their own values with regard to social and
environmental concerns. Effective reporting on sustainability matters is crucial to
investors and potential investors in making decisions to buy, hold or sell shares.
Traditionally investors made these decisions after considering financial performance
and the requirement for companies to prepare financial statements facilitates this
process. Although the basis for investors’ investment decisions has evolved, there is
still no requirement in IFRS Standards for sustainability reports and therefore
investors are reliant on companies preparing reports on a voluntary basis in order to
provide them with the information that they need. Failure of companies to provide
these reports results in an information gap and the possibility that investors will take
their money elsewhere.
It is also true that effective sustainability reporting benefits shareholders by improving
company performance. In order to report on sustainability effectively, Rochester will
be required to monitor and measure its sustainability performance on an ongoing basis.
This accountability will encourage management to make better decisions regarding
the use of resources which will in turn lead to cost savings (e.g. reductions in energy
use) as well as the associated non-financial benefits. A focus on risks is relevant to
sustainability reporting and by deepening its understanding of these risks and
managing them better, Rochester is less likely to be affected by them.
Measuring and managing non-financial impacts also drives innovation in operational
development. All of these are benefits to the company, however these in turn provide
benefits to the owners of the company, the shareholders. Company performance may
be further improved because a focus on environmental and social issues may attract
high-calibre employees, managers and sportspeople to Rochester as well as an
improved reputation amongst customers and suppliers, leading to improved sales and
better buying power.
The fact that Rochester is moving towards operating a more sustainable business may
have a short-term negative financial impact in terms of more cash
outflows and potentially lower profits due to the initial costs associated with
sustainability initiatives. This will reduce EBITDA and increase the level of net debt.
It should, however be remembered that additional performance measures are not
regulated by IFRS Standards and it is normal practice for companies to highlight and
exclude any unusual or non-recurring costs from them. Some of the set-up costs
relating to new initiatives and extra narrative reporting may be identified
as non-recurring and Rochester may choose to exclude them from published
EBITDA.
The managing director appears to have adopted a short-term view and may not
appreciate that in the medium to longer term the company’s investment in
sustainability and sustainability reporting is likely to create financial benefit in the
form of an increase in Rochester’s enterprise value. The managing director also
appears to be focusing solely on financial performance and financial performance
measures. She has not appreciated the positive impact that these changes will have on
society, the planet and the economy. These factors are increasingly important to all
stakeholders and this non-financial positive impact is likely to translate into a
financial benefit through increased investment, access to more markets and so on.
source_doc: ACCA:sbr:essay:SBR-2022-Pre June-A.pdf#3

stem_zh:
Q4
(a) (i) Renovation costs
Branwell should capitalise the costs of the extra floor in accordance with IAS 16 and
depreciate these costs over the six years of the lease. The requirement to restore the
property at the end of the lease term to the conditions required by the terms and
conditions of the lease is often referred to as a dilapidation clause. IFRS 16 Leases
recognises that an obligation for those costs may be incurred at the lease
commencement date or as it progresses. Such costs are recognised as part of the cost
of the right-of-use asset when the obligation for the costs arises and the obligation is
measured and recognised in accordance with IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent
Liabilities and Contingent Assets.
The expected costs of removing the additional floor should be recognised as a
provision when the obligating event occurs (i.e. when the floor is added), providing
that a reliable estimate of the costs can be made. The corresponding accounting entry
is an increase to the carrying amount of the right-of-use asset. The right-of use asset,
including the restoration costs, is depreciated over the remainder of the lease term.
As regards the disrepair of the building, the estimated costs should be spread over the
six years of the agreement. IAS 37 would indicate that Branwell has a present
obligation arising from the lease agreement because the landlord can
recharge the costs of any repair to Branwell. The obligating event is the
wear andtear to the building which will arise gradually over the tenancy period and its
repair can be enforced through the legal agreement. The obligation relates to wear
and tear and is not related to future operating costs. The wear and tear will result
in at ransfer of economic resources and a reliable estimate of the yearly
obligation will be made, although itwill not necessarily equate to one sixth per year.
This amount will increase the provision that is already recognised in respect of
dilapidations and is added to the carrying amount of the right-of-use asset, which is
then depreciated over the remaining lease term.
(ii) Revenue
Branwell is providing Parson with rights to use its intellectual property (its designs
and patented processes) under licence.The promises to provide these are not distinct
because they are interdependent within the context of the contract. Therefore the
contract includes a single performance obligation.
IFRS 15 guidance on licensing arrangements applies if there is an up-front payment.
The transaction price is fixed at $200,000 and there is a single performance obligation;
the key accounting issue is therefore whether revenue is recognised at a point in time
or over time.
A performance obligation relating to licensing is satisfied over time if the arrangement
provides access to the seller’s intellectual property as it exists throughout the licence
period. It is satisfied at a point in time if the arrangement provides access to the
seller’s intellectual property as it existed when the licence was granted.
IFRS 15 goes on to specify that revenue is recognised over time if all of the following
criteria are met:
1. The seller is required or expected to undertake activities that affect the intellectual
property to which the licensee has rights. In this case the licence does not provide
Parson with access to any new designs, however it does entitle Parson to details of the
expected updates to manufacturing processes.
2. The licensee is exposed to positive or negative effects of the seller’s activities that
affect the intellectual property. Here it can be argued that Parson’s success in
manufacturing and retailing Branwell branded footwear is directly linked to
Branwell’s activities in marketing the brand and engaging influencers to promote the
brand on social media.
3. Any activities that the seller undertakes in relation to the promised designs and
processes does not result in the transfer of goods or services to the licensee. Here
further activities by Branwell in relation to the intellectual property are not separate
performance obligations.
Therefore where a fixed payment is made at the start of the arrangement, a contract
liability is recognised and transferred to be recognised as revenue over time on a
straight line basis. The contract is cancellable after 12 months and so the transaction
price of $3 million should be recognised as revenue at $250,000per month for the first
year of the contract. No further revenue is recognised in the second year should the
contract not be cancelled.
The second payment option is a sales-based royalty. IFRS 15 provides specific
guidance on this form of consideration. Revenue is recognised at the later of:
• When the sale or usage occurs, and
• When the performance obligation to which all or some of the sales-based royalties
relate has been satisfied.
Therefore under this option Branwell should recognise revenue as Parson makes sales.
Since revenue is contingent upon the level of sales achieved by Parson, Branwell
should only recognise royalty revenue when it is probable that it will be received.
This will normally be when sales have occurred. Practically Parson may be required
to report its sales monthly or quarterly to Branwell and receipt of this sales
information will allow Branwell to recognise revenue.
(b)Flood
The flood occurred in December 20X3, in the month preceding the year end of 31
December 20X3. Some costs had been incurred by the reporting date, however other
matters remained unresolved.
Branwell has incurred significant costs in conducting a clean-up operation during the
financial year. These costs should be expensed as incurred and are therefore
recognised in full in the statement of profit or loss for the year ended 31 December
20X3. These costs are non-recurring in nature and the management of Branwell may
therefore choose to present them as exceptional items by presenting them as a single
line item in the statement of profit or loss as permitted by IAS 1. The aim of such
presentation is to provide more useful information to users.
The flood has caused some building foundations to become unstable, indicating that
the building used for packaging operations is impaired and its carrying amount should
be written down to recoverable amount. This building is unlikely to generate cash
flows that are independent of those from other assets and therefore the impairment
test should be conducted for the cash-generating unit that the building belongs to.
This may be the property as a whole or it may be the whole manufacturing site. Any
impairment should be recognised in the financial statements at 31 December 20X3; if
testing is not complete by the reporting date, the determination of recoverable amount
after the reporting period (but before the financial statements are authorised for issue)
is an adjusting event as it provides evidence of year end conditions. Any impairment
loss should be recognised in profit or loss.
By the year end Branwell has engaged a contractor to build a replacement property for
packaging operations, however work will not commence until February 20X4.
There is no present obligation to pay the contractor at 31 December 20X3, since no
work has yet been performed, and therefore no provision can be made for the
expected costs. The commencement of the building work is not an adjusting event
after the reporting period. The cost is likely to be material and therefore, Branwell
should disclose the capital commitment.
Branwell has budgeted a loss for the manufacturing site for the financial year ending
31 December 20X4. IAS 37 prohibits the recognition of a provision for future
operating losses due to the lack of a present obligation as a result of a past event.
The announcement of an increase in tax rates occurs after the reporting date but the
announcement is assumed to be before the financial statements are authorised for
issue. IAS 10 is clear that an announcement of a change in tax rate is a non-adjusting
event after the reporting period. Branwellshould disclose its effects if they are
considered to be material. The rate increase is 2% and therefore this is unlikely to be
the case.
(c) Reclassification
Reclassification adjustments are amounts recycled to profit or loss in the current
period which were recognised in OCI in the current or previous
periods. On reclassification,these are presented as reversed through OCI in the
same period as they are presented in profit or loss, so there is no overall effect on total
comprehensive income. An example of items recognised in OCI which may be
reclassified to profit or loss are foreign currency gains on the translation of a foreign
operation and gains or losses on cash flow hedges. These are reclassified when the
foreign operation is disposed of and when the hedge is realised.
Those items which may not be reclassified are changes in a revaluation
surplus under IAS 16 Property, Plant and Equipment, and remeasurements of
a defined benefit plan under IAS 19 Employee Benefits. Gains and losses on debt
investments measured at fair value through OCI are reclassified to profit or loss on
the disposal of the debt investment; similar amounts in respect of
equity investments are not reclassified.The interaction between profit or loss and OCI
is unclear to many users of financial statements, especially the notion of
reclassification and when or which OCI items should be reclassified.
A common misunderstanding is that the distinction is based on
realised versus unrealised gains. There are several arguments for and against
reclassification:
• If reclassification ceased, there would be no need to define profit or loss, or any
other total or subtotal in profit or loss, and any presentation decisions could be left to
specific IFRS Standards.
• Reclassification protects the integrity of profit or loss and provides users with
relevant information about a transaction which occurred in the period.
• Reclassification can improve comparability where IFRS Standards permit similar
items to be recognised in either profit or loss or OCI.
Those against reclassification argue that the recycled amounts add to
the complexity of financial reporting, may lead to earnings management and the
reclassification adjustments may not meet the definitions of income or expense in the
period as the change in the asset or liability may have occurred in a previous period.
The lack of a consistent principle for determining how items should be presented has
led to an inconsistent use of OCI in IFRS Standards. Opinions vary but there is a
feeling that OCI has become a home for anything controversial. Many users are
thought to ignore OCI, as the changes reported are not caused by the operating flows
used for predictive purposes.
The IASB’s Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting has sought to
clarify what distinguishes items of income and expense which are presented in profit
or loss from those presented outside profit or loss (i.e. in OCI). Because the statement
of profit or loss is the primary source of information about financial
performance for the period, all income and expenses are, in principle,
included in that statement.
The Board may, however, decide that in exceptional circumstances income or
expenses arising from a change in the current valueof an asset or liability (i.e.
notbased on historical cost) are to be included in OCI (to provide more relevant
information or a more faithful representation of financial performance for the
currentperiod).
In principle, income and expenses included in OCI in one period are reclassified from
OCI to the statement of profit or loss in a future period when
doing so results in more relevant information or provides a more faithful
representation of financial performance for that futureperiod. However, if there is no
clear basis for identifying that future or the amount that should be reclassified, the
Board may decide that income and expenses included in OCI are not to be
subsequently reclassified.
source_doc: ACCA:sbr:essay:SBR-2022-Pre June-A.pdf#4

stem_zh:
Q1
Exhibit 1 Defined benefit pension scheme：
Kostas Co operates a defined benefit scheme for its employees and the cost of
remeasurements for the current year in accordance with IAS 19 Employee Benefitsis
$14 million. This remeasurement cost has been included in administrative expenses.
In order to reduce the risks to which it is exposed, Kostas Co’s directors are
considering reducing the number of participants in the defined benefit
scheme. The company would pay compensation from plan assets to
those participants who are affected.
Exhibit 2 Controlling shareholding in Easton Co：
On 1 May 20X6, Kostas Co acquired 70% of the equity interests of
Easton Co. The purchase consideration comprised cash of $150
million and the fair value of the identifiable net assets of Easton Co
was $160 million at that date. The fair value of the
non-controlling interest (NCI) in Easton Co at acquisition was $54 million. The
share capital and retained earnings of Easton Co were $55 million and $85 million
respectively and other components of equity were $10 million at the date of
acquisition. The excess of the fair value of the identifiable net assets at acquisition
over carrying amount is due to plant, which is depreciated using the straight-line
method and has a five-year remaining life at the date of acquisition.
Kostas Co disposed of a 10% equity interest to the NCI of Easton Co on 30 April
20X8 for a cash consideration of $34 million. The carrying amount of the net assets
of Easton Co at 30 April 20X8 was $210 million before any adjustments on
consolidation. Goodwill has been impairment tested annually and as at 30 April
20X7 had reduced in value by 15% and at 30 April 20X8 had lost a further 5% of its
original value before the sale of the equity interest to the NCI.
Exhibit 3 Financial asset investment：
On 1 May 20X5, Kostas Co purchased a $20 million five-year bond with semi-annual
interest of 5% payable on 31 October and 30 April. The purchase
price of the bond was $21.62 million. The effective annual interest
rate is 8% or 4% on a semi-annual basis. The bond is classified as an amortised
cost financial asset in accordance with IFRS 9 Financial Instruments.
During the preparation of the consolidated financial statements at the start of June
20X8, the financial controller identified that the interest due on the bond at 30 April
20X8 had not been received. She observed that the October 20X7 interest had been
paid almost four weeks after the due date and is concerned that the asset may be
impaired. She is considering changing the classification of the asset to fair value
through other comprehensive income(FVTOCI) in order to avoid having to test for
impairment.
Exhibit 4 Potential acquisition of shares：
The directors of Kostas Co are considering the purchase of an equity stake in another
company, Weston Co, however they are concerned that the investment is of a risky
nature. They are looking to acquire a 25% holding initially and if the investment
proves to be successful then they will look to acquire a further 50% of the equity
shares, to give a holding of 75%.
Requirements:

stem_zh:
Q2
Exhibit 1 Ethical issues and investment property:
A trainee member of the accounting department has proposed to Ms Smeaton, the
head accountant and an ACCA member, a number of initiatives aimed at increasing
Heathcliff Co’s profitability for the current period.
The trainee is the nephew of Heath cliff Co’s chairman, whose annual bonus is based
on reported profit.After the trainee accountant proposed the initiatives, an email was
accidently circulated around the company stating that if the initiatives were
implemented the trainee would receive 20% of any bonus paid to the chairman.The
trainee has proposed that a property owned by Linton Co, a subsidiary of Heathcliff
Co, should be classified as investment property. The property is rented
to Lucas, another of Heathcliff Co’s subsidiaries. The trainee accountant
argues that by classifying the property as investment property, any gains in value
will be included in profit or loss but any fall in fair value can
be deferred by including in other comprehensive income.
Exhibit 2 Useful life of assets:
The trainee has suggested that the useful life of all plant and equipment should be
increased from five years to ten years. The trainee argues that this will increase the
annual profit dramatically and,as the asset has already been paid for,noone is bothered
about how much depreciation is charged for the assets.
Exhibit 3 Inventory write down:
The trainee has proposed classifying a large write-down in inventories that occurred
in the current period as a prior period adjustment. The trainee argues that the
inventories were probably impaired over 12 months ago,inthe year ended 31
December 20X8, and therefore it was a mistake of the previous period not to write
down the inventories.
Exhibit 4 Loan notes:
The trainee suggests recognising a substantial discount on loan notes acquired in the
year as income in profit or loss. The loan notes were issued by one of HeathcliffCo's
suppliers. The supplier had been experiencing significant financial difficulty
which explains the deep discount below nominal value on issue of the loan
notes.
Requirements:

stem_zh:
Q3
Exhibits 1 Borrowing costs:
On 1 February 20X9 Rochester commenced construction on a new stadium, for its
own use to host sporting events. Construction continued until the stadium was
completed,which was after the year end of 31 May20X9. The direct costs were $20
million in February 20X9and then $50 million in each month until the year end.
Rochester has not taken out any specific borrowings to finance the construction of the
stadium, but it has incurred finance costs on its general borrowings during the period,
which could have been avoided if the stadium had not been constructed.
Rochester has calculated that the weighted average cost of borrowings
for the period1 February to 31 May 20X9 on an annualised basis
amounted to 9% per annum.
Exhibits 2 Player registrations:
Rochester purchases and sells players’ registrations on a regular basis. Rochester
must purchase registrations for that player to play for the club. Player
registrations are contractual obligations between the player and Rochester. The
costs of acquiring player registrations include transfer fees, league levy fees, and
player agents’ fees incurred by the club. Often players’ former clubs are paid
amounts which are contingent on the performance of the player whilst they play for
Rochester. For example, if a contracted basketball player scores an average of more
than20 points per game in a season, an additional $5 million may become payable to
his former club. Also, players’ contracts can be extended and this incurs additional
costs for Rochester.
At the end of every season, which also is the financial year end of Rochester, the club
reviews its playing staff and makes decisions whether they wish to sell any players’
registrations. These registrations are actively marketed by circulating other clubs with
a list of players’ registrations and their estimated selling price. Players’ registrations
are also sold during the season, often with performance conditions
attached. Occasionally, it becomes clear that a player will not play for the club again
because of, for example, a player sustaining a career threatening
injury or being permanently removed from the playing squad for
another reason. The playing registrations of certain players were
sold after the year end, for total proceeds, net of associated costs,
of $25 million. These registrations had a carrying amount of $7 million.
Exhibits3 Sustainability reporting:
Rochester has recently started taking actions to reduce its carbon footprint and operate
in a more environmentally friendly way by, for example, installing solar panels on the
roof of its new stadium. It has also launched a number of ‘people initiatives’ including
training schemes for the unemployed in communities in which it operates. The
finance director of the company is keen to report to stakeholders on the company’s
sustainability activities and would like to incorporate narrative on environmental and
social issues into the next annual report. The managing director has, however,
indicated that she is concerned that the initiatives pursued by Rochester and the move
towards more narrative reporting will negatively affect the company’s published
performance measures, specifically EBITDA and net debt.
Requirements:
source_doc: ACCA:sbr:essay:SBR-2022-Pre June-Q.pdf#3

stem_zh:
Q4
Exhibits1 Renovation costs:
Branwell leased its head office during the current accounting period and the
agreement terminates in six years’ time. There is a clause in the lease relating to the
internal condition of the property at the termination of the lease. The clause states
that the internal condition of the property should be identical to that at the outset of
the lease. Branwell has improved the building by adding another floor to part
of the building during the current accounting period. There is also
a clause which enables the landlord to recharge Branwell for costs relating to the
general disrepair of the building at the end of the lease.
Exhibits2 Licensing arrangement:
To expand its market base Branwell intends to enter into a two-year arrangement with
an overseas company, Parson Co whereby Parson will manufacture and sell some of
Branwell’s designs under licence. The licence may be cancelled by either party after
12 months. Branwell will provide Parson with its designs and details of the
manufacturing processes to make its patented ‘Barefoot’ soles. Parson will not be not
entitled to manufacture any new designs that Branwell releases over the course of the
contract, however Branwell will provide details of updates to manufacturing
processes in relation to existing designs. Branwell continually works on improving its
manufacturing processes to save costs and improve output. The agreement has not yet
been finalised because Branwell has not decided on the form of consideration. It will
either require Parson to pay Branwell a single up-front fee of $3million, or 15% of its
net revenues from the sale of the footwear each quarter.
Exhibits3 Flood:
At the start of December 20X3, flooding caused extensive damage to one of
Branwell’s manufacturing sites.A clean up operation, which incurred significant cost,
removed debris and excess water from the site by 20 December,however engineers
identified that the foundations of an older building on the site, which was used for
Branwell’s packaging operations, had become unstable. Management decided on 22
December to build a new replacement property on the site to house its packaging
operations,using more robust weather and flood resistant materials and design. A
contractor was engaged at the end of December and there build was expected to start
in February20X4. The work would take an extended period of time and mean that
Branwell’s manufacturing capacity would be reduced. Accordingly the company’s
accountants budgeted a loss for the manufacturing site for the year ended 31
December 20X4. In March20X4, the government announced that the flooding was
related to climate change and was expected to become a more regular occurrence. It
therefore intended to construct flood barriers along rivers and would increase
company tax rates by 2% to raise funds for this purpose.
Requirements:

stem_zh:
Q1
Sterling Co is currently preparing its individual and consolidated financial
statements for the year ended 31 March 20X9 in accordance with International
Financial Reporting Standards.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the question:
1. Acquisition of Berthold Co - describes the purchase of 40% of Berthold Co
on 1 January 20X7 and the further acquisition of 35% on 1 December 20X8.
2. Investment in Malting Co - describes the purchase of 30% of Malting Co on
1 July 20X8.
3. Batch Co: discontinued operation - describes the acquisition of Batch Co
which Sterling Co decides to sell on 1 January 20X9.
1. Acquisition of Berthold Co
Sterling Co paid $25 million for 40% of Berthold Co’s 10 million $1 ordinary
shares on 1 January 20X7 when Berthold Co’s retained earnings were $18
million. Berthold Co has no other reserves. Sterling Co exercised significant
influence over Berthold Co’s financial and operating policy decisions.
A further 35% stake in Berthold Co was acquired on 1 December 20X8 for $41
million, when the fair value of Berthold Co’s identifiable assets and liabilities
was $55·3 million, and Berthold Co’s retained earnings were $42·3 million. The
difference between the fair value of the identifiable assets and liabilities of
Berthold Co and their carrying amounts related to non-depreciable land. The
market price of Berthold Co’s shares just prior to 1 December 20X8 was $9·20
per share. Sterling Co measures non-controlling interest at fair value at the
date of acquisition. Goodwill at 31 March 20X9 is not impaired.
Berthold Co reported a profit for the year ended 31 March 20X9 of $11·1
million.
2. Investment in Malting Co
On 1 July 20X8, Sterling Co paid cash of $2·5 million and issued loan notes
with a nominal value of $1·1 million and a fair value of $1·3 million to acquire
30% of the equity interest of Malting Co. The remaining 70% of the equity in
Malting Co is owned equally between two unrelated companies. All key
operating decisions require unanimous consent of all three investing parties.
Each of the three investing parties has the right to its share of the net assets of
Malting Co via a contractual agreement. Malting Co reported a loss of $0·8m
for the year ending 31 March 20X9 and a dividend was neither paid nor
proposed. Because of the poor performance in the year, all three investing
parties provided separate guarantees to Malting Co’s bank。
3. Batch Co: discontinued operation
On 1 January 20X9, Sterling Co announced a plan to dispose of Batch Co,
which operates in a significantly different business sector to the rest of the
group.
Sterling Co first acquired 70% of the equity shares in Batch Co on 1 January
20X7 for consideration of $3·1 million when the fair value of the identifiable net
assets acquired was $3·6 million. The ordinary share capital and retained
earnings of Batch Co were $0·5 million and $2·1 million respectively. The
excess of the fair value of the identifiable net assets was due to a building with
an estimated useful life of 10 years at the acquisition date. The fair value of the
non-controlling interest (NCI) in Batch Co was $0·9 million on 1 January 20X7.
Sterling Co uses fair values to measure NCI.
On 1 January 20X9, Batch Co reported retained earnings of $2·9 million, with
no change in ordinary share capital, or impairment of goodwill, since
acquisition.
Sterling Co decided to treat Batch Co as a disposal group held for sale in
accordance with IFRS5 Non-current Assets Held for Sale and Discontinued
Operations. Assets and liabilities of Batch Co require no remeasurement to
meet applicable IFRS standards prior to restating as a disposal group, and the
total fair value less costs to sell of the disposal group at 1 January 20X9 was
estimated to be $4·4 million.
Requirements:
(a) Using exhibit 1:
(i) explain how Berthold Co will be accounted for, including calculations, in
Sterling Co’s consolidated Statement of Profit or Loss for the year ended 31
March 20X9; and(8 marks)
(ii) explain and show how the goodwill in Berthold Co would be calculated at 1
December 20X8.(6 marks)
(b) Using exhibit 2, advise the directors of Sterling Co, with illustrative
calculations, how the 30% investment in Malting Co should be accounted for in
Sterling Co’s individual and consolidated financial statements.(7 marks)
(c) Using exhibit 3, explain to the directors of Sterling Co how to account for
Batch Co as a discontinued operation at 1 January 20X9 in Sterling Co's
consolidated financial statements. Your answer should include:
• a calculation of the goodwill at 1 January 20X7; and
• a calculation of how the investment in Batch Co should be measured prior
to disposal. (9 marks)
source_doc: ACCA:sbr:essay:SBR-2022-Sep&Dec-Q.pdf#1

stem_zh:
Q2
Jassie Co operates in the oil and gas industry. The current financial year end
of Jassie Co is 31 December 20X7.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the question:
1.Granting of licences - describes the process of granting licences to
customers.
2.Ethical issues - describes the potential ethical issues surrounding the
working practices of the finance director.
1.Granting of licences
Jassie Co provides services to customers to help them evaluate whether there
are potential oil reserves in a particular geographical location. Customers
generally enter into the contract before the survey process commences and
eventually purchase a licence to search for oil deposits.
Service phase
During the service phase, Jassie Co collects site data over the first year of the
contract, then processes and analyses the data over the next six months. The
area surveyed is determined by Jassie Co who is the only decision-maker and
the owner of both the data and the land being surveyed. All of the data is made
available to customers in the final survey report. Customers cannot process
the data or obtain a licence other than by Jassie Co’s approval. The value to
the customer is in the granting of the licence and not in the data.
As data is being collected and processed, presentations and reports are given
to customers to indicate the potential of an area. Customers have ongoing
access to the data during collection and processing. When the final report is
made available, the customer decides whether to invest in the area.
Granting of licence
The customers can exit the contract on receipt of the report but the total price
of the contract, including the price of the final licence, is still payable to Jassie
Co. In practice, no customer leaves the contract at this stage. The licence is
granted at this stage and once the licence has been granted, there is no further
analysis or service provided to the customer.
The directors of Jassie Co concluded that the company has only one
performance obligation, being the granting of a licence at the end of a survey.
This is because the collection and processing of the data (service phase) is a
component of the granting of the licence and cannot be distinguished from the
licence. In addition, the service phase alone is of no benefit on its own. The
final licence provided to the customer is based upon the data collected and
processed during the service phase.
2.Ethical issues
Jassie Co uses Big Data analytics and various algorithms in order to indicate
the potential of finding oil in a particular geographical location. The chief data
officer understands the science and statistics, and can explain outcomes
simply and clearly to customers. The finance director, Mr Betso, who is ACCA
qualified, has little knowledge of the technology involved. He feels that the
technology used is too complex and there is no need for him to understand it
as long as the chief data officer understands it. The chief data officer reports to
the finance director.
Recently, the outcome of one set of data showed that there was little chance of
oil being found on one of the sites but the finance director insisted that the data
was run again to include historical data which would then indicate a more
favourable outcome for a customer, Olio Co.
Mr Betso has a friend, Ms Stylos, who works as the accountant at Olio Co. Ms
Stylos has asked Mr Betso for advice as to how to account for the licence
granted to Olio Co. Mr Betso suggested that Ms Stylos use IFRS 16 Leases as
it is a right-to-use licence. In addition, Mr Betso knew that Ms Stylos held
shares in Olio Co and suggested to her that the share price of Olio Co was
likely to fall because there was little chance of oil being found as a result of the
granting of the licence.
Requirements:
(a) Discuss:
• whether revenue from the service phase and the grant of a licence to a
customer are separate performance obligations or a single performance
obligation; and
• whether the revenue should be recognised at a point in time in accordance
with IFRS 15 Revenue from Contracts with Customers.(8 marks)
(b) Explain the ethical issues for the finance director, Mr Betso, setting out how
the issues may be resolved. (10 marks)
Professional marks will be awarded in part (b) for the quality of the discussion
regarding the resolution of the ethical issues. (2 marks)
source_doc: ACCA:sbr:essay:SBR-2022-Sep&Dec-Q.pdf#2

stem_zh:
Q3
Rubul Co is a group which manufactures furniture and is currently preparing its
financial statements for the year ended 31 December 20X7.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the question:
1. Consignment arrangement - describes the sale and despatch of garden
furniture to garden centres and its inclusion in the financial statements.
2.Provision for tax liability - describes the basis of the calculation of sales and
income tax liabilities for the current and previous years.
3. Historic exchange rate - describes the use of the historic exchange rate
which is not updated to disclose financial statement movements.
1. Consignment arrangement
Rubul Co provides garden centres with furniture on a consignment basis.
Rubul Co retains legal title to the furniture until they are sold by the garden
centre to a customer. The products are immediately available for sale on
delivery to the garden centres. The garden centre does not have any obligation
to pay Rubul Co until a sale to a customer occurs. Physical possession
remains with the garden centre unless the furniture is sold or returned to Rubul
Co. Rubul Co also retains the right to have any unsold furniture returned to
them, or to transfer unsold products to another retailer. Once the garden
centre has sold the furniture, Rubul Co has no further obligations. The garden
centres then remit to Rubul Co the sales proceeds net of their agent’s
commission.
Rubul Co has decided to include the sales value of all furniture delivered to
and held by garden centres in revenue.
2.Provision for tax liability
Sales tax
Rubul Co has shipped and sold goods in an overseas country for ten years.
Rubul Co was under the impression that the sales in the overseas country are
subject to sales tax if the company had a physical and economic presence in
the country. Rubul Co had always understood that because it did not have a
physical presence in the country, it was exempt from sales tax. However, the
tax law stated that companies were subject to sales tax if they had a physical
oreconomic presence. Therefore, at 31 December 20X7, Rubul Co has
realised that a liability for sales tax has existed for the previous ten years.
Rubul Co has never collected sales tax or filed sales tax returns in the
overseas country. Rubul Co has never been contacted regarding sales tax by
the tax authorities in the overseas country. Rubul Co therefore considers that
the risk of detection by the taxation authorities is very low. However, if the
taxation authorities knew about Rubul Co’s activities, it is probable that Rubul
Co would be liable for uncollected sales taxes, interest and penalties.
Rubul Co considers that the tax authorities would settle for an amount less
than the full liability if full disclosure was made as it is ‘widely understood’ that
the taxation authority would look back no more than seven years to determine
the amount of sales tax due. The tax authorities can reclaim any sales tax
liability from prior years.
Income tax
On 31 December 20X7, legal proceedings were started against Rubul Co by
the tax authorities in the same overseas country for unpaid income tax. Rubul
Co disputes the fact that it owes income tax as it has always paid this tax on
time. Rubul Co considers that it is just coincidence that these proceedings
have commenced and does not relate to the sales tax issue. Rubul Co
considers that there is little chance of the income tax case succeeding. In any
event, Rubul Co considers that it cannot measure any potential income tax
liability as in its opinion none exists.
3. Historic exchange rate
Rubul Co has substantial deposits in currencies other than its functional
currency. It also has a number of overseas subsidiaries where the local
currency is used to prepare the financial statements which are then translated
into the presentation currency under IAS 21 The Effects of Changes in Foreign
Exchange Rates.These countries often suffer fluctuations in exchange rates,
so Rubul Co decided to eliminate the effect of exchange rate differences and
presented financial information using a single historic fixed exchange rate for
the translation of the international subsidiaries, which is not updated between
period
Requirements:
(a) Explain, in accordance with IFRS 15 Revenue from Contracts with
Customers, how the consignment arrangement should be dealt with in the
financial statements of Rubul Co and explain why the proposed treatment of
revenue is not in accordance with IFRS 15.(7 marks)
(b) Assess whether Rubul Co should create a provision in accordance with IAS
37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets for its sales tax
and income tax liabilities in the financial statements for the year ended 31
December 20X7.(10 marks)
(c) Discuss:
• the advantages for the users of the financial statements of applying a single
historic fixed exchange rate in the financial statements of Rubul Co;
• whether this is acceptable practice under IFRS standards; and
• whether it would enhance the usefulness of the financial statements if the
foreign exchange gains or losses on Rubul Co’s overseas deposits were
recognised in other comprehensive income.(8 marks)
source_doc: ACCA:sbr:essay:SBR-2022-Sep&Dec-Q.pdf#3

stem_zh:
Q4
Juan Co is a group of companies which operates in the energy industry. The
financial year end of the company is 31 December 20X7.
The following exhibit, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provides
information relevant to the question:
1. Climate change issues - outlines the issues which Juan Co currently faces
due to the impact of climate change.
1. Climate change issues
Juan Co has several coal-fired power plants and is currently facing challenges
dealing with the impact of climate change. Juan Co has not considered the
effect of climate change when preparing the current financial statements.
There has been a decline in the demand for the energy from Juan Co’s
coal-fired plants because of the volume of greenhouse gases emitted by the
plants. The company is committed to meeting the expanding climate change
regulations of its jurisdiction relating to emissions. If a company does not meet
the jurisdiction’s climate-related targets, then fines are imposed by the
government for this failure. Juan Co is also considering a restructure to
achieve eco-design of its products and services and to improve the energy
efficiency of its existing buildings.
As a result, Juan Co has had to change and adapt its business activities and
operations, including increased expenditure on research and development.
The company has revised its assumptions about future profitability and cash
flows based upon it cutting its emissions. If it does not cut emissions, it will risk
losing many customers.
Juan Co’s supply chains are becoming more complex and globally spread with
many points of possible failure. Juan Co has subsidiaries in regions of the
world which are subject to weather extremes and anticipates some physical
harm to its property, plant and equipment because of this. There is the
likelihood that some of the power plants will require decommissioning sooner
than expected which will mean restoring the land damage. Some restoration
work may not be possible at the level required by regulatory changes.
Corporate risk is increasing due to environmental damage caused by climate
change.
Investors are worried about their share value in Juan Co because of an
anticipated increase in carbon taxes and the significant costs which the
company will have to incur if it is to meet its commitments.
In the financial statements for the year ended 31 December 20X7, Juan Co
has carried out impairment tests and has not recognised an impairment loss.
Requirements:
(a) Many preparers of financial statements feel that there is sufficient
disclosure of climate-related matters in the management commentary and
therefore there is no need to disclose any further information in the actual
financial statements, especially if there is no impact on those financial
statements. Even though there is an acceptance that investors feel such
information is important, some preparers think that the materiality decision
does not extend to climate change as there is no IFRS standard dealing with
the subject
Discuss the validity of the above views expressed by preparers of financial
statements regarding materiality.
Note: you do not need to refer to any exhibit when answering part (a).(5 marks)
(b) Discuss the potential effects of climate-related matters on the financial
statements of Juan Co for the year ended 31 December 20X7 in applying the
following IFRS standards.
(i) IAS 16 Property, Plant and Equipment and IAS 38 Intangible Assets (6
marks)
(ii) IAS 36 Impairment of Assets (6 marks)
(iii) IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets(6 marks)
Professional marks will be awarded for the overall understanding of
climate-related issues in corporate reporting. (2 marks)
source_doc: ACCA:sbr:essay:SBR-2022-Sep&Dec-Q.pdf#4

SBR Pre -December 2024 PP Mock – Answers
Whitaker Co - Suggested Solution:
(a) Christon
According to IFRS 11 Joint Arrangements, a joint arrangement is an arrangement of
which two or more parties have joint control.
Joint control is the contractually agreed sharing of control of an arrangement, which
exists only when decisions about the relevant activities require the unanimous
consent of the parties sharing control.
A contract between parties is not required to be a formal, written document and the
documented discussions between Whitaker and Allister are sufficient.
These state that decisions about the relevant activities of Christon require an 85%
majority. This can only be achieved by Whitaker and Allister voting together. This
means that unanimous consent is required, and that Whitaker and Allister jointly
control Christon.
A joint arrangement is classified as a joint operation or a joint venture. A joint
operation is a joint arrangement in which the parties that have joint control have
rights to the assets of the arrangement and obligations for its liabilities.  A joint
venture is a joint arrangement in which the parties that have joint control have rights
to the net assets of the arrangement.
Since neither Whitaker nor Allister have rights to Christon’s assets nor are they liable
for its debts, this is a joint venture rather than a joint operation.
(b) Impairment of Kennedy
In accordance with IAS 36 Impairment of Assets, Kennedy is impaired if its carrying
amount exceeds recoverable amount. Recoverable amount is the higher of value in
use of $3.8 million and fair value less costs of disposal of $3.85 million. It is therefore
$3.85 million.
The carrying amount of Kennedy should be measured on a basis which is consistent
with value in use and fair value less costs to sell. Therefore, carrying amount should
exclude current assets and total liabilities.
It should include Kennedy’s non-current assets which are recognised in the
consolidated financial statements, including the fair value adjustment related to land,
plus the goodwill arising on the acquisition of Kennedy.
Since the non-controlling interest (NCI) was initially measured as a proportion of the
fair value of the identifiable net assets when Kennedy was acquired, recognised
goodwill of $318,000 represents parent goodwill only. Notional goodwill attributable
to the NCI should also be included in the carrying amount of Kennedy for impairment
testing purposes.
The carrying amount of the Kennedy CGU is therefore:
$’000
Non-current assets 3,760
Fair value adjustment - land 190
Recognised (parent) goodwill 318
Unrecognised (notional NCI) goodwill
($318,000 x 30%/70%)
4,404
The carrying amount exceeds recoverable amount by $554,000 ($4.404m - $3.85m).
The loss should be allocated firstly to goodwill (both recognised and unrecognised),
which absorbs $454,000 of it ($318,000 + $136,000)
The remaining loss of $100,000 is then allocated to other assets within the scope of
IAS 36. Financial assets are not within the scope of IAS 36 and therefore none of the
impairment loss is allocated to these. The loss is allocated to property, plant and
equipment and intangible assets in proportion to their carrying amounts:

(c) Investment in Freeman
Freeman is already a subsidiary of Whitaker and therefore the purchase of additional
shares should be treated as a transaction between the owners of the company.
Goodwill is not recalculated, and no profit or loss arises on the transaction.
The carrying amount of the NCI at 31 December 20X5 should be reduced to reflect
the transfer of the additional 30% equity shares to the Whitaker Group, and any
difference between the amount of this reduction and the $1.25 million paid to acquire
the additional shares should be recognised in equity attributable to the owners of the
parent company.
The carrying amount of the NCI at 31 December 20X5 is:

Since consideration for the additional shares was $1.25 million, the difference of
$372,500 should be recognised as a loss directly in group equity.

Devonald - Suggested Solution:
(a) Land and buildings
Land is a class of property, plant and equipment (PPE) and should be recognised
and measured in accordance with IAS 16 Property, Plant and Equipment. PPE is
initially measured at cost, including purchase price, costs attributable to bringing the
asset into the location and condition intended for use.
Cost also includes the initial estimate of the costs to decommission or restore the
asset and the site it is on, if there is a present obligation to do so as required by IAS
37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets.
The initial measurement of the land should therefore include the cost of the land, the
expenditure to line the quarry with clay and the cost of dividing the site into 20 waste
cells.
Devonald is contractually obliged to pay $40,000 to cap each waste cell when it has
been filled; however this is not a present obligation when the landfill site is acquired
and developed because none of the cells have been used at this stage. Therefore,
the capping costs are not part of the initial measurement of the landfill site.
IAS 23 Borrowing Costs requires that eligible borrowing costs on qualifying assets
are recognised as part of the cost of the asset. Since the landfill site takes an
extended period of time to get ready for use, it is a qualifying asset.
Devonald has used general borrowings to fund the purchase and development of the
landfill site and should recognise related borrowing costs incurred from 1 May 20X5
to 30 September 20X5 as part of the cost of the site. These are calculated by
applying a weighted average interest rate to borrowed funds used specifically on the
landfill site.
The landfill site became operational on 1 October 20X5 and was used for 3 months
until the year end. Ordinarily land is not depreciated because it is not consumed,
however in this case the economic benefits associated with the land are consumed
as the cells built into the quarry are filled up with waste.
Therefore, the landfill site should be depreciated over its 20-year useful life, starting
on 1 October 20X5. The depreciation charge for 3 months should be recognised as
an operating expense in profit or loss.
Based on the expected time of one year to fill a waste cell, one cell at the landfill site
will be at 25% of capacity by the reporting date. A provision should therefore be
recognised for the capping costs at this date. The obligation to cap the current cell
being filled exists regardless of how full it is, and the $40,000 cost does not vary
depending on remaining capacity. Therefore, a provision should be recognised for
the full $40,000. A corresponding entry is made to operating expenses in profit or
loss for the period.
The provision is unlikely to require discounting to present value given that the
capping cost will be paid in nine months’ time.
(b) Ethical issues
A student accountant was asked by the finance director to make the accounting
entries in relation to Devonald’s new landfill site and Jac, who has recently joined the
company, has been asked to review these entries at year-end. Jac is unsure of the
correct treatment and has asked for advice.
Both the finance director and Jac are ACCA members and have a duty to comply
with the ACCA Code of Ethics and Conduct.
The finance director asked the student accountant to make accounting entries in
relation to the landfill site without supervision or subsequent timely review. It is
apparent from the student’s work that he did not have the knowledge to complete
this task correctly.
This is unprofessional conduct on the part of the finance director and does not
display the principle of professional behaviour and behaving with courtesy and
consideration. The finance director should have known that the student accountant
was not qualified to make the accounting entries. The finance director should not
have put a corporate hospitality event ahead of supervising the student.
Jac has displayed a lack of integrity by accepting the task to review the student’s
work when she has not kept her technical knowledge up to date. She has not
maintained her level of knowledge to ensure that her employer receives a competent
professional service, and this indicates a lack of professional competence and due
care.
This lack of adherence to ethical principles by Jac appears to be the direct result of a
self-interest threat to objectivity, whereby Jac is worried that she may be asked to
leave Devonald at the end of her probation period.
Actions
As a colleague of Jac I should help her to review the accounting entries made by the
student accountant in order to complete the task at hand and allow the financial
statements to progress.
I should advise Jac to complete technical update and CPD training in order to update
her knowledge of IFRS Accounting Standards. I should encourage her to share her
position with the finance director and other members of the financial reporting team
so that they can support her in this.
If Jac does not agree to undertake technical update training, I should discuss the
issue with her immediate line manager in order to avoid further situations in which
the accounting function might be compromised, and errors might be made in
financial statements.
In respect of the finance director, I should point out that it is inappropriate and unfair
to rely on staff members to complete work which they are not qualified to do.
If the finance director is unwilling to accept this, I should discuss the matter with
other board members or the audit committee and may also contact the ACCA
helpline.
I should keep a record of all conversations with both Jac and the finance director in
case the matter needs to be taken further.
