Professional skills marks 2
Arderinto Co - Suggested Solution
(a) Greenhouse gas emissions
IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets provides strict
criteria which must be met before a provision is recognised. There must be a present
obligation (legal or constructive) as a result of a past event, an outflow of economic
resources must be probable, and the amount of the obligation must be able to be
measured reliably.
Battery energy storage systems
Only obligations which arise from past events that exist independently of an entity’s
future actions are recognised as provisions.
In the case of the battery energy storage systems there is no present obligation to
pay $10 million to the supplier because Aderinto can avoid the future expenditure by,
for example, not placing the order. The fact that Aderinto directors have publicly
announced the intention to buy the systems does not alone create a present
obligation.
Furthermore, there is no probable outflow of economic benefits, because although
Aderinto will have to pay $10 million for the systems, it will receive resources in
exchange (non-diesel power) and will use this to construct properties which it sells or
rents at a profit.
Carbon credits
There is no present obligation to purchase carbon credits in future years until
Aderinto, through its operations, has emitted the greenhouse gases (GHGs) which it
has committed to offset. A present obligation will only exist when it has emitted
GHGs and failed to achieve the 80% reduction in net emissions by other means.
Aderinto was therefore incorrect to make the provision for $13.78 million and should
reverse the accounting entries, reducing expenses and increasing profit for the year.
(b) Apartment building
The apartment building meets the definition of an investment property at the start of
20X5.
According to IAS 40 Investment Property, an investment property is a property which
is held to earn rentals or for capital appreciation or for both. This definition includes a
building which is leased out under one or more operating leases.
During 20X5 the building is transferred to be sold in the ordinary course of sale. At
this time, it meets the definition of inventory.
IAS 40 states that a transfer out of the investment property category takes place
when a property ceases to meet the definition of investment property and there is
evidence of a change in use.
Management intent is not evidence of a change in use and therefore the transfer
does not take place on 1 May 20X5.
IAS 40 indicates that the commencement of development with a view to sale is
evidence of a transfer from investment property to inventories. Therefore, the
transfer takes place on 1 July 20X5.
Since Aderinto measures investment property at fair value, the fair value of the
property at the date of transfer is the deemed cost for the subsequent application of
IAS 2 Inventories.
Therefore, on 1 July 20X5 the property should be remeasured to its fair value of $1.5
million before being transferred to inventories. A loss of $0.1 million should be
recognised in profit or loss.
Inventories are measured at cost. Cost includes the cost of bringing inventories to
their present location and condition. In the case of the apartment building, it includes
the $1.5 million deemed cost of the building and the $350,000 remodelling cost,
totalling $1.85 million.
At the year end, the property remains unsold. It should be measured, in accordance
with IAS 2, at the lower of cost and net realisable value.
Net realisable value is $1.66 million ($1.75m fair value less $90,000 selling costs).
Therefore, the property should be measured at $1.66 million, resulting in an
inventory write down expense of $190,000 in profit or loss ($1.85m - $1.66m).
(c) Amounts paid to social influencer
The amount payable to the social influencer includes a fixed fee plus commission in
relation to apartments in two separate buildings. River Park will be leased to
students and Moor Place will be sold to individual purchasers.
River Park commission
In relation to River Park, IFRS 16 Leases applies. It requires that any initial direct
costs of obtaining an operating lease are recognised as part of the cost of the
underlying asset.
Initial direct costs are the incremental costs of obtaining a lease which would not
have been incurred if the lease had not been obtained.
The total commission payable to the influencer of $30,000 ($500 x 75% x 80) meets
this definition and should be added to the cost of the underlying investment property
when it becomes payable i.e. when lease contracts are signed.
The $30,000 should subsequently be recognised as an expense in Aderinto’s profit
or loss when the related lease income is recognised. Therefore, an expense of
$2,500 should be recognised each month from 1 August 20X5 to 31 July 20X6.
Moor Place commission
In relation to Moor Place, IFRS 15 Revenue from Contracts with Customers applies.
It requires that the incremental costs of obtaining a contract should be recognised as
an asset if the entity expects to recover these costs.
Contract costs recognised as an asset should then be amortised on a systematic
basis consistent with the transfer to the customer of the goods or services to which
the asset relates.
The commission payable to the influencer is an incremental cost of obtaining the
sales contract for 30 apartments and therefore IFRS 15 permits the recognition of
$36,000 (30 x $1,200) as a contract cost asset at 31 December 20X5.
IFRS 15 does, however, allow an entity to recognise incremental costs to obtain a
contract as an expense when incurred if the amortisation period would be less than
one year. It is likely that Aderinto could apply this practical expedient.
If Aderinto does recognise the contract cost asset of $36,000, each $1,200 will be
recognised as an expense in profit or loss when each individual apartment sale is
completed . This is likely to occur at, or shortly after, 31 January 20X6 when
construction is complete.
Fixed fee
The $180,000 fixed fee payable to the influencer relates to the promotion of both
contracts as well as general brand awareness. It is not an initial direct cost as
defined by IFRS 16 or an incremental cost as defined by IFRS 15. Both Standards
require that other costs are recognised as an expense as incurred.
This is supported by the IFRS Accounting Standards which apply to the underlying
properties themselves:
• IAS 40 Investment Property applies to River Park and it requires that an
investment property is initially measured at purchase price plus directly
attributable expenditure. The influencer fixed fee is not directly attributable to
the development of the investment property.
• IAS 2 Inventories applies to Moor Place and it requires that inventories are
measured at the cost of bringing inventories to their present condition and
location. IAS 2 explicitly excludes selling costs from being costs of inventories.
The fixed fee should be recognised at $60,000 ($180,000/3 months) per month,
resulting in an expense of $120,000 in the year ended 31 December 20X5.
Conclusion
Therefore, Aderinto was incorrect to recognise a selling asset of $246,000 and
amortise it over 30 years. In the year ended 31 December 20X5, it should have
recognised:
• $30,000 as part of the cost of River Park, and
• $120,000 as an expense in profit or loss.
It may have recognised $36,000 related to Moor Place as an expense or an asset.

Kasim - Suggested Solution
(a) Pension scheme
Kasim’s accountant has calculated the net pension surplus at 31 December 20X5
without taking account of the past service cost. Additional pension benefits have
been provided and therefore the past service cost results in an additional expense in
profit or loss and a decrease to the net pension surplus.
After taking account of the past service cost, the calculated pension surplus at 31
December 20X5 is $4.3 million ($5.5m - $1.2m).
IAS 19 Employee Benefits requires that the defined benefit pension amount
recognised in the statement of financial position is the net position after the present
value of the defined benefit obligation is deducted from the fair value of plan assets.
Application of the above results in a net pension surplus of $3.2 million ($19.1m -
$15.9m).
The difference between the year-end balance calculated by Kasim’s accountant (and
adjusted for the past service costs) and the year-end balance as advised by
actuaries should be recognised as a remeasurement in other comprehensive
income. It is presented as an amount which will not be reclassified to profit or loss.
The remeasurement loss is calculated as $1.1 million ($4.3m - $3.2m).
IAS 19 restricts the amount which can be recognised as a pension surplus to the
asset ceiling. This is the present value of any economic benefits available in the form
of refunds from the plan or reductions in future contributions to it.
Kasim’s pension surplus is therefore restricted to $2.6 million.
The $0.6 million write down from the actuary-advised surplus of $3.2 million to the
asset ceiling test of $2.6 million is recognised as an extra remeasurement loss in
other comprehensive income which will not be reclassified to profit or loss.
(b) Coffee beans and futures contract
Kasim has purchased inventory of coffee beans. In accordance with IAS 2
Inventories, it should recognise these initially at their cost of $4.54 million (1m x
$4.54) on 1 December 20X5.
Kasim entered into the futures contract to hedge changes in the value of its inventory
and designated this as a fair value hedge. In accordance with IFRS 9 Financial
Instruments, the hedged item is the inventory of coffee beans, and the hedging
instrument is the futures contract.
In a fair value hedge, the hedged item is remeasured to fair value at the reporting
date and any change in measurement is recognised in profit or loss.
At 31 December 20X5, the inventory is therefore remeasured to $4.9 million (1m x
$4.90) and a gain of $360,000 is recognised in profit or loss.
The derivative has no initial cost and a fair value of nil (since the contract price is
equal to the futures price in the market for delivery on the contracted date).
Therefore, the derivative is not recognised on 1 December 20X5.
At 31 December 20X5, the fair value of futures contract is calculated as follows:
$’000
Contract price (1m x $4.95) 4,950
Futures price obtainable on 31
December 20X5 for delivery on
contracted date
(1m x $5.17)
5,170
_____
Fair value 220
The derivative should be recognised as a financial liability because the price to sell
coffee beans in the contract is less than the price which could be obtained on 31
December 20X5 for delivery on the contracted date.
Kasim should recognise a corresponding $220,000 expense in profit or loss.
The net effect on the statement of profit or loss is therefore a gain of $140,000
($360,000 - $220,000).
(c) Pension surplus
Kasim’s pension asset is unlike other assets in its statement of financial position in
that it is not used directly by Kasim’s management to generate economic benefits. In
addition, the pension is not strictly within the control of Kasim’s management.
As a result of these factors, the pension asset is likely to be less understandable to
investors than other types of non-current asset, such as property, plant and
equipment.
The lack of understandability limits the relevance of recognising a pension asset.
Investors are unlikely to understand that a surplus in the statement of financial
position represents reduced future contributions and may struggle to interpret the
asset as having a positive impact on future cash flows. They are also unlikely to
understand the effect of the pension surplus on profits and so Kasim’s ability to pay
dividends.
In addition, relevance is reduced because the balance has limited predictive
qualities; future years’ balances will be affected by changes in many variables,
including actuarial assumptions and interest rates, as well as plan assets’
performance.
Investors may also fail to understand that a defined benefit pension is administered
by external parties and affected by external factors and therefore does not reflect
management’s ability to run the company.
Hedging arrangement
Kasim applies hedge accounting which aligns the financial statements with Kasim’s
risk management strategy and so may help investors to better understand the
stewardship of management and their ability to manage risk.
Hedge accounting also eliminates volatility from the financial statements: if Kasim did
not apply hedge accounting it would recognise the $220,000 loss on the derivative in
20X5 and subsequently recognise a gain on the sale of the coffee beans which is
$360,000 greater than otherwise (because it would be based on their original cost) in
20X6. By applying hedge accounting Kasim removes this volatility and matches
gains and losses in the same period.
As a result, the financial statements become more relevant to investors who can
better predict future results of the company based on underlying activities.
As a result of the hedging arrangement Kasim’s inventories of coffee beans are
measured at their fair value at the reporting date rather than cost. This provides
more relevant information to investors because more up to date prices indicate how
much future purchases of coffee beans might cost and what the impact on margins
might be. Recognising gains in the period when they occur, rather than in the period
when the sale is made, could also be argued to provide more timely information.
Measurement of inventories at fair value may, however, reduce the comparability of
financial information. This is particularly true because applying hedge accounting
principles is an accounting policy choice; it is not mandatory. Other inventories held
by Kasim, and inventories of coffee beans held by competitors may be measured at
original cost. The fair value gain on Kasim’s coffee bean inventories may be
interpreted by investors as a holding of a greater volume of goods, which in turn may
lead them to expect higher revenues in the future.
Hedge accounting is complex, and many investors will also have difficulty
understanding the accounting treatment applied, including how the net $140,000
gain in profit or loss is calculated. This lack of understandability may outweigh the
benefits of more relevant information.

Q1 Whitaker Co
Whitaker Co (Whitaker) is the parent of the Whitaker Group. All group companies
prepare financial statements in accordance with IFRS Accounting Standards and
have a financial year end of 31 December 20X5.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the question.
1. Investment in Christon – provides information about the purchase of shares
in Christon Co (Christon) during the year.
2. Investment in Kennedy – provides information about Whitaker’s investment
in Kennedy Co (Kennedy).
3. Investment in Freeman - provides information about Whitaker’s investment
in Freeman Co (Freeman).
4. Draft consolidated SOFP – provides a summarised draft consolidated
statement of financial position (SOFP) as at 31 December 20X5.
This information should be used to answer the question requirements within your
chosen response option(s).
Exhibit 1 Investment in Christon
On 1 July 20X5, Whitaker purchased 30% of the equity share capital of Christon at a
cost of $1.9 million. It recorded the shares as a non-current asset measured at cost.
An unrelated company, Allister Co , purchased 55% of the equity share  capital in
Christon on the same date. The remaining 15% of the equity share capital in Christon
is held by several individual investors.
Documented discussions between Whitaker and Allister state that decisions about
Christon’s operations, financing structure and investments require an 85% majority.
Neither Whitaker nor Allister have rights to the assets held by Christon, nor are they
liable for its debts.
On 1 July 20X5 , the fair value of Christon’s net assets was $6  million. In the year
ended 31 December 20X5, Christon reported profits of $1.4 million and reported gains
on debt investments measured at fair value through other comprehensive income of
$600,000. Both amounts accrued evenly throughout the year.
Exhibit 2 Investment in Kennedy
Whitaker acquired control of Kennedy in 20X1 when it purchased 70% of Kennedy’s
equity share capital. Whitaker has identified Kennedy as a cash-generating unit.
At the acquisition date , the fair value of land owned by Kennedy was $190,000 in
excess of its carrying amount  in its individual financial statements . The land is still
owned by Kennedy at 31 December 20X5. Goodwill on the acquisition of Kennedy
was $318,000 . T he non -controlling interest at acquisition was measured at its
proportion of the fair value of Kennedy’s identifiable net assets.
On 31 December 20X5, as a result of new legislation, Whitaker conducted an
impairment test on Kennedy, finding it to be impaired . Kennedy has been included in
the draft consolidated financial statements correctly in accordance with IFRS 3
Business Combinations and IFRS 10 Consolidated Financial Statements ; however,
the impairment has not yet been accounted for.
The value in use of Kennedy was $3.8 million and the fair value less costs of disposal
was $ 3.85 million. The measurement of value in use and fair value less costs of
disposal excluded cash flows associated with current assets and total liabilities, all of
which generate independent cash flows.
Kennedy’s assets and liabilities  in its individual financial statements as at 31
December 20X5 comprised the following:

Financial assets 570

Exhibit 3 Investment in Freeman
Whitaker acquired control of Freeman on 1 January 20X2 when it purchased 60% of
Freeman’s equity share  capital. At this date , Freeman’s recognised net assets in its
individual financial statements were measured at $1.58 million which was equal to fair
value. However, Freeman had not recognised a brand name in its individual financial
statement, which had a fair value of $200,000  on 1 January 20X2 and an estimated
useful life of 20 years.
The non-controlling interest at acquisition was recognised at its fair value of $850,000.
On 31 December 20X5, when the net assets of Freeman had a carrying amount of
$2.42 million in its separate financial statements, Whitaker acquired a further 30%
interest in the equity share capital of Freeman for consideration of $1.25 million.
Whitaker has prepared the draft consolidated financial statements to include Freeman
as a 60% owned subsidiary, in accordance with relevant IFRS Accounting Standards.
It has made no accounting entries in relation to the purchase of an additional 30%
interest, other than to recognise the additional investment at cost.

Q2 Devonald Co
Devonald Co (Devonald) is a waste management company. It prepares its financial
statements using IFRS Accounting Standards. It has a financial year end of 31
December 20X5.
The following exhibit, available on the left -hand side of the screen, provides
information relevant to the question.
1. Purchase of land  – provides information about land purchased by Devonald
during the year.
This information should be used to answer the question requirements within the
response option provided.
Exhibit 1 Purchase of land
Jac is one of a team of ACCA members in the financial reporting department at
Devonald. She is involved in preparing the company’s financial statements for the
year ended 31 December 20X5, ready for the arrival of the external audit team. She
has recently joined the company after several years working outside the
accountancy profession and is currently in a probationary period.
The finance director, who is also an ACCA member, has asked Jac to review
accounting entries relating to a purchase of land. He told her that the financial
reporting team was short staffed when the entries were made and so he asked a
student accountant to make them; however, the finance director was at a supplier
hospitality event and was unable to supervise the student accountant. The student
accountant has subsequently left Devonald and no one has reviewed the entries.
Devonald purchased the land on 1 May 20X5. The land had previously been used to
quarry for slate. Devonald acquired the land including the quarry for use as a landfill
site. It part-funded the purchase and subsequent expenditure on the site using its
pool of general borrowings.
Between May and September 20X5, Devonald incurred expenditure to line the
quarry with clay to prevent waste escaping into surrounding soil. It also divided the
quarry into 20 separate ‘waste cells’, each of which would be filled, in turn, with
household waste. It is expected that each cell will take one year before it is full .
Devonald’s operating licence requires that waste cells must be capped once full and
the land restored to grassland. This must take place as soon as a cell is no longer in
use. The cost of capping each cell is $40,000.
Devonald started using the new landfill site on 1 October 20X5.
The student accountant recognised the landfill site at cost. This included the
following elements:
• the purchase price of the land;
• the cost of lining the quarry;
• the cost of dividing the quarry into cells; and
• the total capping cost of $800,000.
The student accountant wrote a note stating that the asset should not be depreciated
because land is never depreciated.
Jac is unsure whether this is the correct treatment and has asked you, her colleague,
to help. She has asked you not to tell the finance director that she needs your advice
because she is worried that he will find out that she has not kept her knowledge of
IFRS Accounting Standards up-to-date. Jac is concerned that the finance director
may ask her to leave the company at the end of her probation period.
Requirements:
(a) Using exhibit 1, and in accordance with IFRS Accounting Standards,
explain how to correct the accounting treatment of the land and
associated costs in Devonald’s financial statements for the year ended
31 December 20X5.
(10 marks)
(b) Using exhibit 1, discuss the ethical issues arising from the scenario and
outline any actions which you should take to resolve these.
(8 marks)
Professional skills marks will be awarded in part (b) for the relevance of the ethical
principles and actions identified.
(2 marks)
(20 marks)

Q3 Aderinto Co
Aderinto Co (Aderinto) constructs residential buildings to sell and rent out. It prepares
its financial statements using IFRS Accounting Standards. It has a financial year end
of 31 December 20X5.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the question.
1. Greenhouse gas emissions – provides information about Aderinto’s
commitment to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
2. Apartment building – provides information about one of the apartment
buildings held by Aderinto.
3. Social influencer – provides information about a contract between Aderinto
and a social influencer.
This information should be used to answer the question requirements within your
chosen response option(s).
Exhibit 1 Greenhouse gas emissions
In May 20X5, at Aderinto’s Annual General Meeting, the directors announced to
shareholders that the company was committing to reducing its Scope 1 net
greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 80% by 31 December 20X9. The directors also
announced two ways to achieve this:
• Most GHG emissions on construction sites are related to the burning of diesel
fuel. Aderinto will therefore invest in battery energy storage systems for use
on its construction sites to reduce reliance on energy from diesel fuel.
Aderinto intends to order several of these systems at a total cost of $10
million, for delivery between 20X6 and 20X7. The directors have stated that
despite this investment, all construction projects are still expected to make a
profit.
• To the extent that the use of battery energy storage systems does not reduce
Scope 1 net GHG emissions by 80% compared to the current levels, Aderinto
will buy carbon credits in each of the years 20X6 to 20X9 inclusive. It will buy
these on the final day of each accounting period. These are expected to cost:
$’m
31 December 20X6 1.45
31 December 20X7 1.12
31 December 20X8 0.76
31 December 20X9 0.45
3.78
In its draft statement of financial position at 31 December 20X5, Aderinto has
recognised a provision for the total costs of $13.78 million associated with its
strategy to reduce GHG emissions.
Exhibit 2 Apartment building
Aderinto constructs apartment buildings. It sells some of these and holds the others
to rent to university students. Green Bank is an apartment building which has been
rented to university students.
On 1 May 20X5, Aderinto’s management decided that it would not rent the Green
Bank apartment building to students in the next academic year and would instead
remodel the building and sell it as a single property in the ordinary course of
business. The remodelling started on 1 July 20X5, after all students had vacated the
building, and cost a total of $350,000. Aderinto began to market the building for sale
on 1 October 20X5. The building remained unsold on 31 December 20X5. Selling
costs are expected to be $90,000.
The building originally cost $1.1 million, and it had a carrying amount of $1.6 million
on 1 January 20X5. Its fair value was determined as follows:
1 May 20X5 $1.4 million
1 July 20X5 $1.5 million
1 Oct
ober 20X5
$1.9 million
31 December 20X5 $1.75 million
Aderinto measures owner-occupied property using the revaluation model and adopts
a useful life of 30 years for buildings. It measures investment property using the fair
value model.
Exhibit 3 Social influencer
A social influencer is an individual with credibility in a particular industry and a large
following on social media.
On 1 November 20X5, Aderinto engaged a social influencer to build awareness of its
brand and to promote new apartments in two buildings, River Park and Moor Place,
on social media platforms.
Construction of both buildings began on 1 October 20X5 and is expected to be
completed on 31 January 20X6.
Aderinto intended to lease the 80 apartments in River Park to university students
under 12-month operating lease contracts running from 1 August 20X5 to 31 July
20X6. It intended to sell the 60 apartments in Moor Place to individual purchasers in
the ordinary course of business.
The contract with the social influencer had a term of three months and Aderinto
agreed to pay them:
• An initial fixed fee of $180,000
• $500 for each River Park apartment lease contract signed as a result of the
influencer’s promotional activities.
• $1,200 for each deposit received on a Moor Place apartment as a result of
the influencer’s promotional activities.
The variable fees will be paid at the end of the social influencer’s contract, on 31
January 20X6.
By 31 December 20X5 Aderinto had signed contracts to rent all the River Park
apartments for the next academic year. It could identify that 75% of these contracts
were the direct result of the influencer’s activities. It had also taken customer
deposits on all the Moor Place apartments and could identify that 50% of these sales
were the direct result of the influencer’s activities.
Aderinto has recognised a ‘selling asset’ in its statement of financial position at 31
December 20X5, measured at $246,000, being the full amount payable to the social
influencer. It intends to amortise this over the 30-year useful life of a property,
starting on 31 January 20X6.
Requirements:
(a) Using exhibit 1, and in accordance with IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent
Liabilities and Contingent Assets, explain whether Aderinto was correct
to recognise a provision for the costs of its strategies to reduce
greenhouse gases emissions in its financial statements for the year
ended 31 December 20X5.
(5 marks)
(b) Using exhibit 2, and in accordance with IFRS Accounting Standards,
explain how Aderinto should account for the apartment building in the
year ended 31 December 20X5.
(10 marks)
(c) Using exhibit 3, and in accordance with IFRS Accounting Standards,
evaluate the accounting treatment applied to the amounts paid to the
social influencer in the year ended 31 December 20X5.
(10 marks)
(25 marks)

Q4 Kasim
Kasim Co (Kasim) operates a multinational chain of coffee houses. It prepares
financial statements in accordance with IFRS Accounting Standards. It has a
financial year end of 31 December 20X5.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the question.
4. Pension plan – provides information about a pension plan run by Kasim.
5. Coffee beans – provides information about inventories of coffee beans held
by Kasim.
This information should be used to answer the question requirements within your
chosen response option(s).
Exhibit 1 Pension plan
Kasim operates a defined benefit pension plan for employees. Kasim’s accountant
has calculated the net pension surplus at 31 December 20X5 to be $5.5 million and
included this in the draft statement of financial position. This was calculated by
adjusting the net pension surplus at 1 January 20X5 for benefits paid out, the current
service cost, contributions to the scheme and net interest on the surplus.
Kasim’s actuarial advisers have provided the following additional information to
Kasim’s accountant:
• The fair value of pension assets at 31 December 20X5 was $19.1 million;
• The present value of the defined benefit obligation at 31 December was $15.9
million;
• At the reporting date, pension benefits were increased and this gave rise to a
past service cost of $1.2 million;
• No refunds are available in respect of the surplus; however, the present value
of reductions in future contributions amounts to $2.6 million.
Exhibit 2 Coffee beans
As well as operating coffee houses, Kasim roasts and packages coffee beans and
sells them under its own brand through supermarkets.
On 1 December 20X5, Kasim bought 1 million kilograms of coffee beans for $4.54
per kilogram. To hedge the risk of a decrease in the value of the coffee beans, it
simultaneously entered into a futures contract to deliver 1 million kilograms of coffee
beans on 31 March 20X6 for $4.95 per kilogram.  The contract will be settled net in
cash and Kasim did not pay to enter the contract. Kasim has completed all
necessary hedging documentation and has elected to apply fair value hedge
accounting to the arrangement.
At 31 December 20X5, Kasim still holds all of the coffee beans purchased on 1
December 20X5. On this date, the market price of a kilogram of coffee beans was
$4.90 and the price available in a futures contract to deliver coffee beans on 31
March 20X6 was $5.17 per kilogram.
Requirements:
(d) Using exhibit 1, and in accordance with IAS 19 Employee Benefits,
explain how Kasim should correct the accounting treatment applied to
the defined benefit pension plan.
(5 marks)
(e) Using exhibit 2, and in accordance with IFRS Accounting Standards,
explain, with calculations, how Kasim should account for the inventory
of coffee beans and the futures contract.
(8 marks)
(f) Using exhibits 2 and 3, and with reference to the Conceptual Framework
for Financial Reporting, discuss whether the following provide useful
information to Kasim’s investors:
• the recognition of a defined benefit pension surplus as an asset in
the statement of financial position, and
• Kasim’s choice to apply hedge accounting to the futures contract
and coffee beans.
(10 marks)
Two professional skills marks will be awarded in part (c) for clarity of explanation and
consideration of investor needs.
(2 marks)
(25 marks)

Q3
Jarid Co prepares financial statements in accordance with IFRS Accounting
Standards. The financial year end is 31 March 20X3.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the question:
1. Server contract – explains a contract that Jarid Co entered into with
Servicio Co on 1 April 20X2 to install a server.
2. Cryptocurrency – describes the purchase of cryptocurrency by Jarid Co on
1 January 20X3 and how directors are considering measuring it.
This information should be used to answer the question requirements within
your chosen response option(s).
Requirements (25 marks)
(a) Using exhibit 1:
• explain, in accordance with IFRS 16 Leases, why the server contract
contains a lease; and
• explain, with calculations, how to measure the carrying amount of the
right-of-use asset and the corresponding lease liability in the financial
statements of Jarid Co as at 31 March 20X3.
(9 marks)
(b)(i) Discuss the reasons why cryptocurrency may not meet the
definition of cash or cash equivalents, or that of a financial asset, but
may meet the definition of an intangible asset.
Note: There is no need to refer to a specific exhibit to answer part (b)(i).
(7 marks)
(b)(ii) Using exhibit 2:
· discuss the steps that Jarid Co should take to measure the fair
value of the cryptocurrency in accordance with IFRS 13 Fair Value
Measurement at 31 March 20X3; and
· briefly explain the impact of the fair value measurement on Jarid
Co’s financial statements.
(9 marks)

stem_zh:
Q4
Anchali Co prepares financial statements in accordance with IFRS Accounting
Standards. The financial year end is 31 December 20X8.
The following exhibits, available on the left-hand side of the screen, provide
information relevant to the question:
1. Restructuring – describes the restructuring plan of Anchali Co and its
potential impact on investors.
2. Benefits – describes the benefits payable to employees because of a
restructure.
This information should be used to answer the question requirements within
the response option provided.
Requirements (25 marks)
(a) Using exhibit 1:
(i) explain, in accordance with IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities
and Contingent Assets, why Anchali Co has met the definition of a
restructure;
(3 marks)
(ii) discuss how and when a constructive obligation for the restructuring
costs was created. Your answer should explain why the constructive
obligation was not created on the other dates outlined in exhibit 1;
(5 marks)
(iii) explain how the restructuring costs should be measured and
recognised in the financial statements for the year ended 31 December
20X8. Your answer should explain the implications for goodwill relating
to the acquisition of Stencil Co; and
(5 marks)
(iv) discuss why it is important that Anchali Co should communicate the
restructure to investors and the key points that they should
communicate.
(5 marks)
Professional marks will be awarded in part (a) for clarity and quality of
discussion regarding the restructure and the importance of the communication
with investors.
(2 marks)
(b) Using exhibit 2, and in accordance with IFRS Accounting Standards,
explain, with calculations, how the benefits should be accounted for in
the financial statements of Anchali Co in the years ended 31 December
20X8 and 20X9.
(5 marks)

stem_zh:
Q1
Kabelo Co is the parent company of a group whose financial year end is 31 December
20x5.
Kabelo 公司为集团母公司，财务年底是 x5 年 12 月 31 日。
题干
explanation_zh:
1. Acquisition of Trudos Co
Kabelo Co acquired 80% of the 100,000 equity shares of Trudos Co on 30 June 20X5.
The consideration consisted of a cash payment of $5 per share acquired and an issue
of one Kabelo Co $1 equity share for every four shares acquired in Trudos Co.
Kabelo 公司在 x5.6.30 收购了 Trudos 公司 100,000 支股票中的 80%的股份(也就是
80,000 支股票)。收购对价包括现金对价(每股 5 块钱)以及以股换股(每收购 4 股
需发行 1 支 K 公司的股票进行交换，股票股本为每股$1.)
Kabelo Co’s policy is to value non-controlling interest at fair value at the date of
acquisition. The fair value of Kabelo Co’s and Trudos Co’s shares on 30 June 20X5
were $13 and $8 respectively.
Kabelo 公司采取 fair value 也就是 full goodwill method 计量 NCI(即按照收购日当
天 NCI 的股数*收购日当天子公司的股价进行计算)。收购日当天母公司及子公司
票价分别为每股$13 以及$8($8 用来计算 NCI 在收购日当天的 FV)。
The carrying amount of the net assets reported by Trudos Co on 30 June 20X5 were
as follows:
收购日当天子公司净资产的账面价值如上表。
The only fair value adjustment on acquisition related to plant which had a fair value
of $50,000 above its carrying amount.
收购日当天的唯一公允价值调整为子公司的 plant，公允价值比账面价值高出
$50,000.
The group pays tax at 30% and deferred tax was correctly accounted for within the
consolidated statement of financial position. The taxation figure in the statement of
profit or loss for the year ended 31 December 20X5 is $385,600 and this figure has
been included as taxation paid within the draft statement of cash flows.
集团按照税率 30%交税，同时递延所得税已经被正确计入合并资产负债表里(这
里提示需要考虑递延所得税，因此 plant 的 FV 调整也需要考虑递延所得税的影
响，账面价值增加，但 tax base 不变)。X5 年当年确认了$385,600 的税费进入利
润表，同时也按照此金额作为 tax paid 挤入了现金流量表草表里(此处记账有误，
tax expense 与 tax paid 并不是一个概念，因此需要自行推导 tax paid 的准确金额)。
The group’s financial controller has accurately completed the consolidated
statement of financial position and consolidated statement of profit or loss and
other comprehensive income but the consolidated statement of cash flow is in draft.
The financial controller has not yet considered the impact of the acquisition of
Trudos Co on the consolidated statement of cash flow.
集团的财务负责人已经准确地编制了合并资产负债表以及合并综合性收益表(说
明期末合并资产负债表里已经包含了子公司的信息)，但是合并现金流量表还属
于草稿状态。同时负责人并未将收购子公司对于合并现金流的影响考虑进去。
The statement of cash flows figures for movements in inventories, trade receivables,
trade payables and property, plant and equipment have been calculated by
considering simply the differences in the year-end balances in the consolidated
statement of financial position from 31 December 20X4 and 31 December 20X5.
现金流量表中针对存货、应收账款、应付账款以及 PPE 仅考虑了期初到期末余额
的变动差异数。
Goodwill of Trudos Co was impaired during the year. There were no other goodwill
impairments within the group.
子公司的商誉今年出现了减值。集团其他商誉并未减值。
The depreciation charge of the group for the year ended 31 December 20X5 was
$625,060. There were no disposals of non-current assets by the group during the
year although there were some additions paid for by cash.
集团当年的折旧费用为$625,060。未处置任何非流动资产，但有部分现金收购的
非流动资产。(这部分都需要在现金流量表中进行调整)
Extracts from the consolidated statement of financial position of the Kabelo Group:

Goodwill 447,400 441,100
Deferred tax liability 130,000 250,000

给出合并资产负债表的相关信息。
Requirements 解析:
(a) Using exhibit 1:
(i) adjust the pre-populated spreadsheet to prepare revised extracts for the operating
and investing activities of the consolidated statement of cash flows for the Kabelo
Group for the year ended 31 December 20X5; and (14 marks)
使用 exhibit 1 案例信息，针对预编制现金流量表中的经营性以及投资性活动现金
流进行调整。
(ii) explain the adjustments required to correct the operating and investing activities
of the consolidated statement of cash flows for the Kabelo Group for the year ended
31 December 20X5. (10 marks)
使用文字解释上述的调整过程。
答案解析:

W2 - Tax paid $

Tax expense for x5.12.31 385,600 当期发生数，exhibit 1 给到的信息
DTL@x5.12.31 -115,000
重估增值导致的 DTL 余额已经包含进 SOFP
中，但并不影响 expense，因此需要扣除，
130,000-15,000=115,000。
CTL@x5.12.31 -364,300 期末合并资产负债表信息

W3 - Goodwill impairment $

在 CFO 中加回
Goodwill @x5.12.31 447,400 期末合并资产负债表信息
W4 - Cash purchase of PPE $ 案例信息中提到有现金购置，因此也需
要计算

Depreciation -625,060 Exhibit 1 中给到的信息
